examples.title: Display Environment Variables
examples.description: Various commands to display environment variables
examples.code:
# Display all environment variables
env

# Display specific variable
printenv HOME
printenv PATH

# Using echo
echo $HOME
echo $USER

# Display using set (shows shell vars too)
set | head -20
examples.explanation: env shows all environment variables, printenv shows specific ones, echo with $ accesses variable values, set shows all variables including shell local variables

examples.title: Set and Export Environment Variables
examples.description: Commands to create and modify environment variables
examples.code:
# Create shell variable (local only)
myvar="hello"

# Export to make it environment variable
export myvar

# Or do both in one step
export NEWVAR="world"

# Modify existing variable
export PATH=$PATH:/home/user/bin

# Remove variable
unset myvar
examples.explanation: Shell variables need export to become environment variables. export command marks variables to be inherited by child processes. unset removes variables.

examples.title: Persistent Environment Variables
examples.description: How to make environment variables persist across sessions
examples.code:
# Add to ~/.bashrc for current user
gedit ~/.bashrc
# Add these lines:
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-11
export PATH=$PATH:$JAVA_HOME/bin

# Reload the file
source ~/.bashrc
# or
. ~/.bashrc

# For system-wide (all users)
sudo gedit /etc/profile.d/custom.sh
examples.explanation: Files like ~/.bashrc, ~/.profile, /etc/profile are sourced at login. Adding export commands here makes variables available in every new shell session.

examples.title: Temporary Environment Modification
examples.description: Run commands with modified environment temporarily
examples.code:
# Run command with empty environment
env -i /bin/sh

# Run command with modified PATH only
env PATH=/usr/bin:/bin ls

# Run command with additional variable
env MYAPP_HOME=/opt/myapp myapp

# Compare environment with and without
env | grep PATH
echo "---"
env PATH=/custom/path env | grep PATH
examples.explanation: env -i clears all environment variables. Prepending VAR=value before a command runs that command with modified environment without affecting current shell.
